data.operatingAgency: Kansas Dept. for Children and Families
data.eligibilityCriteria: Current or former foster youth whose case was open after age 16
data.programNotes: State-run independent living services: life-skills coaching, financial literacy, housing search support, monthly stipend. Benefits stop the day the youth turns 21, regardless of whether housing or income is stable. Close to 4 in 10 participants lose contact with the program within the first 90 days post-exit.

data.operatingAgency: Kansas Dept. for Children and Families
data.eligibilityCriteria: Foster youth actively enrolled in postsecondary education or vocational training
data.programNotes: Funds up to $5,000 per year for tuition, books, and education-related expenses. Enrollment verification required every semester. Underutilized by eligible youth because the application is not widely advertised and the paperwork burden scales poorly with housing instability.

data.eligibilityCriteria: Former foster youth who aged out of care while in Kansas custody
data.programNotes: Medicaid coverage extended to age 26 for former Kansas foster youth with no income test. Loss of coverage is overwhelmingly driven by missed recertification notices that got sent to old addresses. Re-enrollment requires an in-person DCF visit, which is the step where most disenrolled youth give up.

data.eligibilityCriteria: Youth aging out of foster care or formerly in foster care seeking permanent connections
data.programNotes: Permanency and belonging model — matches youth with trained volunteer family mentors for multi-year relationships. Not housing. The lowest dropout in the system because the relationship is not age-capped or funding-capped. Biggest constraint is mentor recruitment, not program design.

data.programName: TRAIL (Transitioning Resources for Advancing Independent Living)
data.eligibilityCriteria: Foster youth preparing for independence or recently aged out
data.programNotes: Independent-living skill building — budgeting, cooking, lease negotiation, interview prep — through weekly group sessions plus individual coaching. Operates parallel to DCF IL and frequently serves youth who have fallen off DCF rolls. Still age-capped at 21.

data.eligibilityCriteria: Homeless or at-risk youth, including foster youth in acute crisis
data.programNotes: Community's only 24-hour low-barrier youth shelter. Immediate intake; case management toward housing, education, employment. About 35% of exits occur without a confirmed next placement because BRIDGES and apartment-based options downstream are saturated.

data.eligibilityCriteria: Youth aging out of foster care or homeless youth with an active case plan
data.programNotes: Apartment-style transitional housing on the CrossRoads campus. Youth hold a case plan and work toward permanent housing. Parenting youth with one child accepted. Only eight units exist — demand eclipses supply by an order of magnitude.

data.eligibilityCriteria: Foster youth, at-risk youth, and families in active crisis
data.programNotes: Therapeutic foster-care support and independent-living prep. Trauma-informed counseling, educational advocacy, placement-stability work. Serves a younger entry point than most transition programs, which means some youth roll into TRAIL or DCF IL after aging through EmberHope.
